coredata.dc:title: Effective and environmentally friendly Co nanocatalyst on sodium borohydride hydrolysis in different solvents
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Onat, Erhan
Ekinci, Selma
Şahin, Ömer
İzgi, Mehmet Sait
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the development and performance of a cobalt (Co) nanocatalyst supported by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for hydrogen production via sodium borohydride (NaBH₄) hydrolysis. The CQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method from caffeine and subsequently used to support the cobalt catalyst. The structure and properties of the synthesized catalyst were characterized using techniques such as X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDX). The catalytic performance was evaluated in various solvents, with the highest hydrogen production rate observed in ethanol. The optimal conditions for Co@CQDs catalyst synthesis were identified as an 8-h hydrothermal treatment with a 3 mmol metal loading. The catalyst demonstrated excellent reusability, maintaining 100% hydrogen production efficiency after seven cycles. The activation energy for the hydrolysis reaction was found to be lower for catalysts synthesized in ethanol (25.48 kJ mol−1) compared to those synthesized in water (35.57 kJ mol−1), indicating enhanced catalytic activity. Under optimum conditions, the hydrolysis reaction in the presence of Co@CQDs (water) catalyst produced 23753 mL min−1 g−1 of hydrogen, while the hydrolysis reaction in the presence of Co@CQDs (ethanol) catalyst produced 28751 mL min−1 g−1. This study highlights the potential of CQDs-supported Co nanocatalysts for efficient and sustainable hydrogen production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sodium borohydride
Carbon quantum dots
Caffeine
Green synthesis

Elucidation of the structures of catalysts
Experiments on hydrolysis and the repeated application of catalysts

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The TOF values for NaBH4 hydrolysis catalyzed by caffeine CQDs supported catalyst synthesized in pure water and ethanol was measured as 2943.4 h\u22121 and 3539.6 h\u22121 respectively.This study was supported by the Scientific Research Projects (BAP) Coordination Office of Siirt University with the project number of 2022-S\u0130\u00DCFEB-010.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption spectra (a) and fluorescence spectra (b) of CQDs (caffeine) and Co@CQDs, (c) the optical band transitions of CQDs, Co@CQDs and Co@MOF-CQDs
EDX analysis of a) Co@CQDs (water) b) Co@CQDs (ethanol).
XPS spectra of a) Co@CQDs (water), b) Co@CQDs (ethanol), c) Co/Co@CQDs (water), d) Co/Co@CQDs (ethanol), e) O/Co@CQDs (water), f) O/Co@CQDs (ethanol).
XRD patterns of a) Co(0) b) Co@CQDs (water) c) Co@CQDs (ethanol).
TEM and SEM images of a)Co(0), b) CQDs(Cafein), c)Co@CQDs (water) d) Co@CQDs (ethanol), e) Co@CQDs-water (SEM), f) Co@CQDs-ethanol (SEM).
Factors affecting catalyst synthesis; a) support loading type, b) hydrothermal time, c) metal ratio, d) different solvents.
The effect of NaOH concentration on hydrolysis with a) Co@CQDs (water) b) Co@CQDs (ethanol).
The effect of catalyst amount on hydrolysis with a) Co@CQDs (water) b) Co@CQDs (ethanol).
The effect of NaBH4 concentration on hydrolysis with a) Co@CQDs (water) b) Co@CQDs (ethanol).
The effect of temperature on hydrolysis with a) Co@CQDs (water) b) Co@CQDs (ethanol).
Arrhenius plots of hydrolysis with a) Co@CQDs (water) b) Co@CQDs (ethanol).
The reusability of catalyst in hydrolysis with a) Co@CQDs (water) b) Co@CQDs (ethanol).
The comparison of the catalytic activities of Co@CQDs (ethanol) and Co@CQDs (water) catalysts with several other catalysts.

head.ce:title: Effective and environmentally friendly Co nanocatalyst on sodium borohydride hydrolysis in different solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Siirt University, Siirt, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the development and performance of a cobalt (Co) nanocatalyst supported by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for hydrogen production via sodium borohydride (NaBH₄) hydrolysis. The CQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method from caffeine and subsequently used to support the cobalt catalyst. The structure and properties of the synthesized catalyst were characterized using techniques such as X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDX). The catalytic performance was evaluated in various solvents, with the highest hydrogen production rate observed in ethanol. The optimal conditions for Co@CQDs catalyst synthesis were identified as an 8-h hydrothermal treatment with a 3 mmol metal loading. The catalyst demonstrated excellent reusability, maintaining 100% hydrogen production efficiency after seven cycles. The activation energy for the hydrolysis reaction was found to be lower for catalysts synthesized in ethanol (25.48 kJ mol−1) compared to those synthesized in water (35.57 kJ mol−1), indicating enhanced catalytic activity. Under optimum conditions, the hydrolysis reaction in the presence of Co@CQDs (water) catalyst produced 23753 mL min−1 g−1 of hydrogen, while the hydrolysis reaction in the presence of Co@CQDs (ethanol) catalyst produced 28751 mL min−1 g−1. This study highlights the potential of CQDs-supported Co nanocatalysts for efficient and sustainable hydrogen production.

ce:section.ce:para: Interest in sustainable and renewable energy sources has grown significantly in response to climate change and the growing depletion of fossil resources. This has promoted the creation of novel technologies that guarantee the security of energy worldwide and reduce their negative effects on the environment [1–4]. By lowering reliance on fossil fuels, the utilization of renewable energy sources, including hydrogen, wind, solar, biomass, and sunlight, helps reduce carbon footprints. Simultaneously, advancements in technology leading to greater energy efficiency and the growth of renewable energy infrastructure are essential for creating a sustainable future that is both economically and environmentally sound [5–9]. Because of its many benefits, hydrogen is regarded as one of the highest-quality fossil fuel substitutes, primarily because burning hydrogen only releases water vapor, which is good for the environment since it does not contribute to greenhouse gas emissions [10,11]. Furthermore, obtaining hydrogen from diverse sources, such as biomass and water electrolysis, can enhance energy independence [12]. With its many uses, hydrogen can take on the role of fossil fuels in a variety of contexts, including industrial, transportation, and energy production. Hydrogen is therefore essential for a future powered by renewable energy. Overcoming the obstacles of storage and steady release is essential to the widespread use of hydrogen as an energy source [13,14]. When hydrogen is stored in gaseous form, vast amounts are needed because of its low molecular weight and high diffusion rate. This calls for the creation of affordable and secure storage options, particularly for widespread application in the transportation industry [15–17]. Numerous storage techniques, each with unique benefits and drawbacks, are being researched, including metal hydrides, liquid hydrogen, and compressed hydrogen gas. One of the most important steps toward using hydrogen in energy infrastructure more widely is optimizing existing storage systems [18,19].

Inorganic boron hydrides are among the compounds with great potential for hydrogen production via hydrolysis. Boron hydrides, especially sodium borohydride, can produce large amounts of hydrogen gas when reacted with water. This reaction is generally carried out at room temperature and using simple catalysts, making it an energy-efficient method [20–22]. The use of boron hydrides as hydrogen carriers offers an attractive option for transportation and portable energy applications due to their high hydrogen density. In addition, the by-products formed after hydrolysis are recyclable and have the potential to provide a sustainable cycle [23,24]. However, further research is required on issues such as cost-effectiveness and system integration for the widespread use of this technology. In addition to sodium borohydride, other hydrogen-containing materials are also utilized in the synthesis and storage of hydrogen, including ammonia borane (NH3BH3), lithium borohydride (LiBH4), and potassium borohydride (KBH4). Each of these materials has different advantages and disadvantages, so the selection of an appropriate hydride depends on the application [25–27].

The use of catalysts in the production of hydrogen via hydrolysis plays a critical role in increasing the efficiency and speed of the reaction. Catalysts enable the release of hydrogen at lower temperatures and faster rates by reducing the activation energy required for the reaction. This reduces energy consumption and increases the overall efficiency of the system [28]. Catalysts made of metals such as platinum, palladium, cobalt, and nickel are widely used in this process. Optimization of catalysts not only increases the reaction rate but also minimizes the formation of by-products, resulting in purer hydrogen. Therefore, the development of effective catalysts is of great importance for sodium borohydride to provide a practical and economical solution for the production of hydrogen via hydrolysis [29,30].

To ensure the efficient use of metal catalysts and improve their performance, support materials are employed. Support materials give metal catalysts a larger surface area, more even catalyst particle distribution, and enhanced stability during reactions [31,32]. Carbon-based materials (activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene) oxide-based materials (Al2O3, SiO2, TiO2, ZrO2) zeolites and polymer-based supports are among some of the commonly used support materials. In addition to offering advantages like lower costs and extending catalytic life, support materials can boost the efficiency of catalysts [33]. Because each support material may be tailored for a particular need, catalysts can be employed in commercial and research-based processes more successfully [34].

Research on cobalt-based catalysts for hydrogen generation continues to advance, focusing on enhancing efficiency and sustainability. A cobalt phospho-boride catalyst synthesized within a MOF structure achieved high hydrogen generation rates and low activation energy due to electron modulation by P and B [35]. CoMo/g-C3N4 catalysts, synthesized via ultrasonic-assisted impregnation, displayed high catalytic activity and stability, with optimal Co: Mo ratios improving performance [36]. Dual-supported Co–Cu–B/g-C3N4/Ni foam catalysts exhibited superior photocatalytic activity for NH3BH3 hydrolysis under visible light, achieving enhanced hydrogen generation rates [37]. A cost-effective Ni–Co@g-C3N4 catalyst synthesized via reduction methods demonstrated high catalytic activity and reusability for KBH4 hydrolysis, highlighting its potential for industrial applications [38]. A carbon sphere-supported Co catalyst enabled efficient hydrogen production from KBH4 hydrolysis, offering an alternative to NaBH4 with consistent kinetic modeling results [39]. Some other studies have investigated various innovative catalysts for hydrogen production via hydrolysis of NaBH4 and ammonia borane, including CoS nanoparticles [40], amorphous Ru–Co(OH)x@GO particles [41], and cobalt-based composites such as Co@NHC [42] encapsulated in nitrogen-doped hollow carbon. These catalysts exhibit impressive hydrogen production rates, low activation energies, and good recyclability, offering promising alternatives to precious metals.

CQDs, which are carbon-based semiconductor nanocrystals at the nanometer scale, are formed by arranging carbon atoms in sp2 or sp³ hybridized structures. Their applications span bioimaging, drug delivery, sensors, photovoltaics, and photocatalysis, benefiting from their low toxicity and simple synthesis. Recent advancements have highlighted their role in enhancing photocatalytic efficiency and solar energy conversion. CQDs are also increasingly used in biomedical applications for in vivo and in vitro imaging, detection, and targeted therapy, showing potential in photodynamic and photothermal therapies. Because of their excellent water solubility, chemical stability, and conductivity, CQDs are economical, adaptable, and environmentally safe nanomaterials that could be used in future technologies [43–47].

The aim of this study is to develop a novel cobalt-based catalyst supported by CQDs for hydrogen production during sodium borohydride hydrolysis. In particular, the catalyst was synthesized in two different solvents and compared in terms of hydrogen production rate, which further increases the contribution of the study. By optimizing the synthesis parameters and solvent selection, this study demonstrates a cost-effective, efficient catalyst system with promising potential to advance hydrogen production technologies.
The following materials were utilized in this study: methanol and ethanol from Tekkim, caffeine and CoCI2.6H2O (99 %) from Sigma-Aldrich, and NaBH4 (≥98.0 %) from Merck.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs from caffeine
ce:section.ce:para: This method involved processing pure water and a certain amount of caffeine in an oven. The best hydrothermal production conditions were determined by examining several combinations of temperature and duration, taking into account the photoluminescence effects under UV light at a wavelength of 360 nm. The 8-h waiting period and 180 °C temperature were determined to be the most ideal parameters for hydrothermal process. In order to purify the obtained CQDs, they were centrifuged (10,000 rpm) for 1 h after being filtered (0,45-μm filter). Finally, this CQDs sample was centrifuged for 60 min at 10,000 rpm.

Support loading processes were carried out using precipitation, impregnation and hydrothermal loading techniques. Co-caffeine, Co@CQDs, and Co@MOF-CQDs (water) are the names of the catalysts that were produced using co-precipitation, impregnation, and hydrothermal techniques, respectively. Uncharged, pure Co(0) was also synthesized with co-precipitation method to make comparison with the others.

The reduction of Co salt was carried out by adding dropwise 10 mmol NaBH4 to 10 ml of solvent at room temperature for the preparation of CQDs-supported Co catalyst. After 36–40 h, cobalt species began to precipitate. At the end of this period, centrifugation was performed at a speed of 5000 rpm. In order to fully precipitate the cobalt species, centrifugation was carried out this time at 10,000 rpm for 45 min after it was determined that the reduction process was finished.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elucidation of the structures of catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Some techniques were used together to comprehensively analyze the structural, chemical and physical properties of Co nanocatalyst. XPS technique was used to analyze the surface chemistry of the nanocatalyst, the oxidation states of the elements and their binding energies, and TEM technique, which is ideal for examining the internal structure, particle sizes and arrangement of the nanocatalyst at high resolution. Additionally, EDX was used to obtain information about the distribution of the constituent elements, and XRD patterns were used to reveal the crystal dimensions and structural regularity of the material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiments on hydrolysis and the repeated application of catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: After the reactor where the experiments would be conducted was filled with water, a burette was connected to the reactor as a gas collection system. First, the catalyst and then a certain amount of NaOH were added to a reaction vessel, and then the reaction vessel was placed in the reactor. NaBH4 was slowly and controlledly added to the reactor's solution to start the hydrolysis reaction. The hydrogen gas released during the reaction accumulates in the gas collection system. The gas volume was measured over time to establish the total amount of hydrogen produced. In NaBH4 hydrolysis applications, the effects of NaBH4 and NaOH concentrations, temperature, catalyst amount, hydrothermal duration, and the metal/CQDs ratio were studied.

The average hydrogen generation rate (HGR) metric was used to assess the catalyst's efficiency. To evaluate repeatability, the catalyst was employed seven times in a row in the experiments. Following each experiment, 90% of the liquid fraction was decanted out and fresh NaBH4 was added.

HGR is calculated as shown in the equation below (Equ.1)
(1)
r =
v
(
m L
)
t
( min )
m
c a t
( g )
The variables in the equation are: V is the total volume of hydrogen; r is the rate; mcat is the catalyst quantity; and t is the time.

Fig. 1 is the graph showing the UV–Vis absorption spectra (a) and fluorescence spectra (b) of CQDs (caffeine) and Co@CQDs. The curve shown in black in Fig. 1 (a) has the highest absorption peak around 270 nm (due to n-π∗ transition of C–O and and the π-π∗ conjugate transition of CC) and the absorbans value is above 1.5. This is the region where caffeine-based CQDs absorb the most UV light. This indicates that the structural and electronic properties of CQDs interact strongly with UV light. The spectrum for CQDs doped with Co is displayed by the red curve. The absorption peak for the Co doping is located at 270 nm, but its absorption value is only about 0.2, making it significantly weaker. This suggests that the metal atoms affect the CQDs' optical characteristics, which reduces their UV light absorption.

The fluorescence spectrum of caffeine-based CQDs is represented by the black curve in Fig. 1 (b). The highest wavelength of light emitted when the CQDs are activated by UV light is shown by the peak at about 350 nm in this curve. It is observed that cobalt doping decreases CQDs' PL intensity. The cobalt-doped CQDs are represented by the green curve, which shows that while the emission intensity changes by metal doping, the fundamental structure of caffeine-based CQDs is maintained [48]. As determined from the calculations using the Tauc plot method, the optical band transitions (Fig. 1(c)) were found to be in the range of 4.18–4.1 eV, 3.9–4.2 eV, and 3.9–4.2 eV for CQDs, Co@CQDs, and Co@MOF-CQDs, respectively [49].

Fig. 2 show the FT-IR spectra of the catalyst samples. The similarities between the FTIR spectra of all samples are obvious. However, there is an increase in peak intensities due to the rise of oxygen-containing functional groups at 1500 cm−1 - 1125 cm−1, particularly for Co@CQDs (ethanol) and Co@CQDs (water) nanocomposites. The Brönsted and Lewis acid areas are represented by the peaks in the FT-IR spectra, which are primarily located in the 1500 cm−1 - 1600 cm−1 ranges. For catalytic applications, the catalysts' Brönsted and Lewis acid sites are crucial. The peak at 1502 cm−1 is more prominent for Co@CQDs (ethanol) and Co@CQDs (water) nanocomposites than for other catalysts. The increase in Lewis acidic sites in the catalysts is also attributed to be partly responsible for the increase in catalytic activity [49,50]. The region between 750 and 1000 cm generally indicates C–H bending vibrations and metal-oxygen (M − O) bonds. Especially cobalt oxide structures give absorption in this region. The FT-IR spectra reveal a peak at 754 cm−1, indicating a successful combination of Co metal ions with the CQDs support material [48].

The qualitative and quantitative (weight percent) distribution of the elements in a sample can be found via EDX analysis. The Co@CQDs catalyst's EDX (energy dispersive spectroscopy) study is displayed graphically in Fig. 3 . The catalyst's structure consists of the elements C, O, N, and Co. The high density of cobalt on the surface is an indication that the cobalt metal has been successfully loaded into CQDs. Compared to the catalyst prepared in water (51.08%), the catalyst prepared in ethanol (56.17%) had a greater cobalt ratio. This may indicate that the ethanol solvent is more effective in cobalt loading. Based on the effects of different solvent media and elemental distribution on cobalt loading, the ethanol solvent can reduce the nitrogen content while increasing the cobalt content [51].

Fig. 4 shows the spectra of two different catalysts analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In Fig. 4(a), the O 1s peaks indicate the presence of oxidized cobalt (Co) or oxygen-containing groups on the surface, while the Co 2p3/2 peak confirms the presence of cobalt and indicates the cobalt binding state. The Co 2p3/2 peak is usually associated with oxide forms such as CoO or Co₃O₄. The chemical compositions (a and b) of Co@CQDs samples in ethanol and water are similar. On the other hand, oxidized forms of cobalt may be more prominent in the sample prepared with pure water (a) [47].

Fig. 5 is about XRD patterns of catalyst. It is evident from all three plots that the Co peak is often located between 40 and 50°. The difference between the three catalysts is in crystallinity and structural regularity depending on the synthesis methods and solution medium. The catalyst obtained by the impregnation method shows a broader peak, while the catalysts synthesized by the hydrothermal method (especially in ethanol medium) have a sharper peak. This suggests that the catalyst synthesized by the hydrothermal method in ethanol medium has a more crystalline structure and may possibly show a higher catalytic activity [52]. In addition, the crystallite size (D) of the Co(0) catalyst was determined by Debye Scherrer equation and found to be 16.1 nm.

TEM images of the catalysts are shown in Fig. 6 (a, b, c, d) , and SEM images of Co@CQDs synthesized in water and ethanol are presented in Fig. 6(e) and (f), respectively. The images show that the catalysts are composed of particles with a specific and regular shape. It is evident from Fig. 6 (a) that there is a Co(0) species aggregation. The carbon quantum dot matrix has a homogenous distribution of Co and CoO nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 6(b–d). When samples are exposed to various solvent conditions, small Co nanoparticles undergo oxidation, which is responsible for the creation of CoO. It should be mentioned that the carbon matrix has nanoscale pores, as seen by the white areas in the TEM photos. The TEM data demonstrate that certain dark and brilliant regions have developed on the composites' surface, signifying the growth of Co particles and the porous structure. The average particle sizes of the following are roughly 17.8 nm, 5.1 nm, 9.2 nm, and 14.4 nm for Co(0), CQDs (caffein), Co@CQDs (water), and Co@CQDs (ethanol), respectively. It can be explained by the fact that both caffeine and carbon quantum dot materials prevent particle aggregation by lowering the average particle size. This finding reduces the aggregation of nanoparticles, promoting the creation of new particles with high catalytic activity.

Fig. 7 (a) shows the H2 production data obtained according to the metal loading method. Co@MOF@DOT (metal loaded catalyst by hydrothermal method) shows the highest performance in terms of hydrogen production rate. This catalyst stands out as the catalyst with the fastest increasing hydrogen production volume over time and reaching the highest level. Thus, hyrothermal approach was used to load metal into the catalyst.

The catalysts were subjected to varying times of hydrothermal treatment, including 2, 4, 8, and 16 h. Fig. 7 (b) also makes it clear that as the catalyst's hydrothermal treatment period increases, more hydrogen is produced. However, it was observed that there was not much difference between the hydrogen volume released by the catalysts obtained at the end of the hydrothermal treatment times of 8 and 16 h. Therefore, the ideal time for the hydrothermal treatment was determined as 8 h. There is, however, always a maximum processing time, and processing over an extended period can have unfavorable consequences, thus a balance needs to be established [53]. Since the amount of hydrogen produced by the catalysts did not show any significant difference after 8–16 h of hydrothermal treatment, the ideal hydrothermal time was determined as 8 h.

Utilizing catalysts made with varying metal ratios (0.5, 1, 3, and 5 mmol) in hydrolysis reactions with fixed quantities of CQDs, the highest hydrogen production was achieved with 3 mmol of metal ratio (Fig. 7 c). Co nanoparticles can be uniformly and finely dispersed over the CQDs surface at a Co ratio of 3 mmol. This can improve the catalyst's efficacy in producing hydrogen by increasing its surface area and, consequently, its active sites. It is possible that there are insufficient nanoparticles on the surface at low ratios like 0.5 and 1 mmol. The surface area may decrease as a result of nanoparticles sticking together and forming bigger clusters at high ratios, such as 5 mmol [54,55].

The effect of different solvents (water, ethanol and methanol) on the activity of the catalyst was examined (Fig. 7 d). The highest hydrogen production rate was achieved as a result of the hydrolysis reaction carried out with the catalyst synthesized in ethanol solvent. CQDs-supported Co catalyst (ethanol) provided faster H2 conversion with a good modification solvent. As a result, the reason why the catalyst synthesized in ethanol solvent is more effective may be due to the improvement of the structural and chemical properties of the catalyst by the use of ethanol. This allows the catalyst surface to become more active and a more stable structure to be formed, thus increasing the hydrogen production rate [56].

After conducting hydrolysis reactions with varying NaOH concentrations (ranging from 2.5 % to 10 % by mass), it was found that using 10 % NaOH by mass produced the highest rate of hydrogen production for both catalysts developed using ethanol and water (Fig. 8 ). NaOH solution accelerates the decomposition of sodium borohydride (NaBH₄) by providing OH⁻ ions in the hydrolysis reaction. 10 % NaOH solution can promote the conversion of sodium borohydride into hydrogen gas more quickly and efficiently by providing sufficient OH⁻ ions in the reaction medium. At lower NaOH concentrations, the reaction rate may be insufficient, while higher NaOH concentrations cause excessive OH⁻ ions to accumulate on the catalyst surface, and 10 % NaOH provides the optimal level of OH⁻ ions [57,58].

When the catalyst containing 15 mg Co was employed for catalysts made in both water and ethanol, the maximum rate of hydrogen production was achieved (Fig. 9 ). It is evident that when catalyst quantities were increased from 10 to 15 mg, the rate of H2 production increased somewhat. This rise was caused by an increase in active sites. The rate decreased, though, when the catalyst dosage was raised to 15 mg. This could be because the product clogged the catalyst surface and the catalyst became saturated [59].

4 % NaBH4 by mass was used to produce hydrogen at the highest rate possible (for water and ethanol) (Fig. 10 ). The rate of hydrogen production was calculated to be 23753 mL/min.g at this value. When the reaction occurs at a higher concentration of NaBH4, a more hydrated by-product is formed, which hinders mass transfer to the catalyst surface and lowers the reaction rate, the solution's viscosity increases dramatically.
Experiments on hydrolysis were conducted at 30, 40, 50, and 60 °C (Fig. 11 ). It makes sense that when temperature rises, the amount of hydrogen produced by the NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction increases.

In order to calculate the Turnover frequency (TOF), catalytic reaction data was used. This approach was based on the amount of Co in the catalyst used in the hydrolysis process and the amount of H2 released over time. Equation (2) was employed to compute the TOF values through the utilization of data that underwent adjustments according to reaction time.
(2)
T O F =
H 2
(
m o l
)
R u
(
m o l
)
.
T i m e
( min )
The TOF values for NaBH4 hydrolysis catalyzed by caffeine CQDs supported catalyst synthesized in pure water and ethanol was measured as 2943.4 h−1 and 3539.6 h−1 respectively.

Fig. 12 show the ln k vs. 1/T graphs (Arrhenius equation) obtained for NaBH4 hydrolysis employing the Co@CQDs catalyst at different temperatures. The Eyring-Polanyi equation was used to calculate ΔH# and ΔS# values, and the Arrhenius equation was used to determine activation energies for the hydrolysis of NaBH4. The calculated values are given in Table 1 . The differences in activation energies and thermodynamic parameters between the Co@CQDs catalysts synthesized in water and ethanol arise from solvent-induced changes in catalyst morphology, solvation effects, and the interaction between the catalyst and the reactants. These factors ultimately influence the reaction kinetics and thermodynamic stability, leading to the observed variations in performance.

The hydrogen production efficiency remained 100 % even after the seventh use, however the catalytic performance slightly declined (Fig. 13 ). The accumulation of NaBO2, a by-product of the reaction, on the catalyst's surface can be used to block active sites, which explains why the rate of H2 production decreased following reusability. This inhibits NaBH4's hydrolysis and has an adverse effect on catalytic activity.

The hydrolysis reaction of NaBH4 via Co@CQDs (water) and Co@CQDs (ethanol) catalysts may involve the following steps: (i) Activation of NaBH4: The cobalt sites in the Co@CQDs catalysts interact with NaBH4, activating the boron-hydrogen bond, making it more susceptible to hydrolysis. (ii) Electron Transfer: The CQDs facilitate electron transfer from the catalyst to NaBH4, aiding in the cleavage of the B–H bond. (iii) Hydrogen Evolution: Water molecules break the B–H bonds, releasing H2 gas while forming sodium borate or its derivatives as by-products. (iv)Stabilization of intermediates: The Co@CQDs support and stabilize reaction intermediates, improving the efficiency and rate of hydrogen release, with the medium (water or ethanol) affecting the catalyst's performance. From the described reaction processes, it can be inferred that half of the generated hydrogen originates from the NaBH4 solution, while the remaining half is derived from water during the hydrolysis process facilitated by the Co@CQDs catalyst in the NaBH4 solution [60,61].

Table 2 highlights the performance of the catalyst synthesized in this study compared to various catalysts used in hydrogen generation systems. The findings demonstrate that the catalyst developed here achieves a remarkably high hydrogen generation rate (HGR) with notably lower activation energy during the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride, showcasing its superior efficiency and potential for practical applications.

This work highlights the successful development of a Co@CQDs catalyst (both in water and ethanol media separately) for efficient hydrogen production via sodium borohydride hydrolysis. The Co@CQDS catalyst synthesized in ethanol showed superior hydrogen production performance compared to those synthesized in water. Key factors, such as optimal hydrothermal time, metal-to-CQDs ratio, and reaction conditions, were determined for maximum hydrogen yield. The optimal hydrothermal time was determined to be 8 h, and a metal-to-CQDS ratio of 3 mmol was found to be the most effective for maximizing hydrogen production for Co@CQDs (ethanol) catalyst. The activation energies for the hydrolysis reactions catalyzed by the Co@CQDs catalysts synthesized in water and ethanol were determined to be 35.57 kJ mol−1 and 25.48 kJ mol−1, respectively. The catalyst synthesized in ethanol also displayed better control over particle size and pore structure, contributing to its enhanced catalytic activity. The catalyst exhibited excellent reusability with a slight decline in performance due to by-product accumulation. This work offers valuable insights into the design of sustainable catalysts for hydrogen production, with future research focusing on optimization and scalability for industrial applications.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Erhan Onat: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Selma Ekinci: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ömer Şahin: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mehmet Sait İzgi: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Scientific Research Projects (BAP) Coordination Office of Siirt University with the project number of 2022-SİÜFEB-010.
